text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Clinical Trial Cycle 4 Assessment | EGFR/MET Bispecific Antibody + SOS1 Inhibitor (Phase II)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Tran is a 64-year-old Vietnamese-American woman with stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, presenting for her 4-cycle on-study assessment following enrollment on a Phase II clinical trial combining an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific monoclonal antibody (IV q2 weeks) with an oral SOS1 inhibitor (daily, 28-day cycle). She reports overall improvement and has been tolerating the regimen reasonably well.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Tran was originally diagnosed approximately 2 years ago with stage IIIB (T3N2M0) right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant by tissue NGS, TP53 co-mutation identified. She was initially treated with osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as first-line targeted therapy given EGFR-driven disease and patient preference to defer upfront chemoradiation. She achieved an initial partial response (primary mass reduced from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm, mediastinal adenopathy cleared) lasting approximately 14 months before developing confirmed radiographic and pathologic progression. At that time, restaging identified growth in the primary RLL mass (3.1 cm), a new right adrenal metastasis, new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and three new brain metastases (right cerebellar 1.1 cm, left frontal 0.8 cm, right parietal 0.6 cm). Resistance biopsy of the adrenal lesion confirmed acquired EGFR T790M mutation as the dominant resistance mechanism (AF 19.4%), with subclonal PIK3CA E545K and low-level CDK4 amplification also noted. Osimertinib was discontinued.

She subsequently underwent Gamma Knife SRS to all three intracranial lesions, followed by second-line carboplatin (AUC 5, later dose-reduced to AUC 4 for grade 3 thrombocytopenia) plus pemetrexed (Alimta) x4 cycles, achieving a partial response in the chest and excellent intracranial control on MRI. She then transitioned to single-agent pemetrexed maintenance while completing clinical trial screening.

She enrolled on the current Phase II trial and received her first infusion of the EGFR/MET bispecific antibody (JBI-602-like construct) plus the oral SOS1 inhibitor approximately 16 weeks ago. Cycle 1 day 15 was complicated by a grade 2 infusion-related reaction (flushing, urticaria, transient hypotension to 88/52 mmHg) that responded to antihistamine and corticosteroid; subsequent infusions have been administered with enhanced pre-medication without recurrence. Protocol-mandated restaging CT/MRI after cycle 2 showed encouraging partial response — the RLL primary decreased from 2.3 cm to 1.7 cm, adrenal metastasis decreased from 1.1 cm to 0.7 cm, and bilateral pulmonary nodules resolved entirely. Intracranially, the previously treated lesions continued to show radiation necrosis/volume reduction with no new CNS disease.

Today she returns after completing cycle 4. She reports:
- Fatigue: Improved compared to the platinum-doublet era; she describes it as manageable, roughly grade 1. She has been able to resume part-time teaching (2 days per week), which she finds deeply meaningful.
- Dyspnea: Substantially improved from her pre-treatment baseline. She can climb one flight of stairs without stopping, compared to being limited on flat ground at initial diagnosis.
- EGFR-class rash: Persistent low-grade acneiform eruption on the face and neck; she reports it as cosmetically bothersome but not painful and has been using topical clindamycin gel consistently.
- GI symptoms: Intermittent grade 1 nausea, particularly on the days she takes the oral SOS1 inhibitor with breakfast rather than after a full meal; she self-adjusted her dosing schedule and this has helped. Denies vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation.
- Peripheral neuropathy: Stable grade 1 fingertip paresthesias from prior carboplatin exposure. No progression, no functional limitation with writing or keyboard use. She is a biology teacher and can still handle fine motor tasks.
- Neurological: No headache, no visual disturbance, no seizure activity, no focal weakness. Neurologically intact on exam.
- Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain.
- No bleeding, bruising, or significant epistaxis since thrombocytopenia episode on carboplatin.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: Grade 1 fatigue, intermittent mild nausea, grade 1 acneiform rash (face/neck), grade 1 fingertip paresthesias (stable).
Negative: No fever, chills, or night sweats. No hemoptysis. No dysphagia. No significant weight loss (patient reports weight stable, actually up approximately 1 kg from prior visit). No headache, seizure, focal neurological deficits. No chest pain. No lower extremity edema. No hematuria. No abdominal pain.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vitals: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, RR 14, SpO2 97% on room air, Weight 56.2 kg (up 1.1 kg from last visit).
General: Well-appearing Asian woman, appears stated age, in no acute distress. Alert and conversational.
Skin: Grade 1 papulopustular/acneiform eruption distributed over the forehead, nasolabial folds, chin, and anterior neck, without open lesions or superinfection. No skin breakdown. Mild xerosis on bilateral lower extremities.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: PERRL. Sclerae non-icteric. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No thrush.
Neck: No supraclavicular, cervical, or axillary lymphadenopathy appreciated.
Chest/Pulmonary: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezing, or pleural rub. Breathing appears comfortable and unlabored at rest.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No JVD.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. Bowel sounds present.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing. Mild residual paronychia of right index finger (grade 1), clean, no purulence.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Gait normal and steady. Upper and lower extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally. Sensation grossly intact to light touch; mild diminished fine sensation at fingertips bilaterally (stable, consistent with prior carboplatin neuropathy).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 5.8 k/uL (reference 4.0-10.5), Hgb 10.9 g/dL (low, stable mild anemia — consistent with prior chemotherapy and chronic disease), Plt 198 k/uL (recovered, within normal limits). ANC 3.1 k/uL (adequate).
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 138, K 4.1, Cl 101, CO2 25, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (CrCl estimated ~68 mL/min, stable). Glucose 94. Total bilirubin 0.6, ALT 22 U/L, AST 18 U/L, Alk phos 74 U/L. Albumin 3.8 g/dL.
TSH: 2.8 mIU/L (stable on levothyroxine 88 mcg daily).
INR: 1.1. No anticoagulation.
Note: Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 10.9) is unchanged from prior visit; attributed to cumulative treatment effect from prior platinum-based therapy. No transfusion threshold met. Reticulocyte count not checked today.

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Most recent protocol CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain were obtained after cycle 2 (approximately 8 weeks ago). Per those results:
- CT chest: RLL primary mass decreased to 1.7 cm (down from 2.3 cm at trial entry); bilateral pulmonary nodules resolved; right adrenal metastasis decreased to 0.7 cm.
- MRI brain: Continued volume reduction at all three previously treated SRS sites (right cerebellar, left frontal, right parietal lesions), consistent with ongoing radiation necrosis/treatment effect. No new intracranial metastases or leptomeningeal disease.
Next protocol-mandated CT/MRI imaging is scheduled in 2 weeks (4-month on-study assessment per trial protocol). Results will be reviewed at next visit. Per patient, she has had no new neurological symptoms to suggest intercurrent CNS progression in the interval.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma with acquired T790M resistance, now on Phase II clinical trial (investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody + oral SOS1 inhibitor), cycle 4 complete.
   - Patient continues to demonstrate clinical benefit with objective partial response seen at the cycle 2 restaging (RLL primary 1.7 cm, adrenal met 0.7 cm, pulmonary nodules resolved). She is clinically improved — weight trending up, dyspnea improved, able to resume part-time work.
   - ECOG PS 1 (functionally improved; arguable PS 0-1).
   - Will proceed with cycle 5 on trial today pending lab review (labs reviewed above; adequate counts and organ function).
   - 4-month protocol imaging (CT C/A/P + MRI brain) scheduled in approximately 2 weeks — we will review results and discuss at next on-study visit.
   - Continue enhanced pre-medication protocol for IV bispecific infusion: diphenhydramine 25 mg IV, acetaminophen 650 mg PO, methylprednisolone 40 mg IV, given the cycle 1 day 15 grade 2 IRR history. No recurrence since enhanced protocol implemented.
   - Patient reminded of trial protocol requirements including adherence to oral SOS1 inhibitor dosing schedule and diary documentation.

2. EGFR-class acneiform rash, grade 1 (face/neck), stable.
   - Remains well-managed on topical clindamycin 1% gel applied twice daily.
   - No systemic antibiotics currently indicated given grade 1 severity.
   - Moisturizer with SPF reinforced. Counseled to avoid harsh cleansers.
   - Reassured that rash activity may correlate with EGFR pathway engagement; no dose modification indicated.

3. Peripheral neuropathy, grade 1, stable (residual from prior carboplatin).
   - No new interventions needed. Vitamin B6 supplementation discussed; patient declines, prefers observation.
   - Will continue to monitor; if worsening, referral to occupational therapy.

4. Mild anemia, grade 1 (Hgb 10.9 g/dL), stable, likely treatment-related.
   - No transfusion needed. Not symptomatic for anemia (no exertional angina, no lightheadedness).
   - Iron studies to be checked at next visit if Hgb drops further.

5. Hypothyroidism, well-controlled.
   - Levothyroxine 88 mcg daily continued. TSH 2.8, within goal range.

6. Hypertension, well-controlled.
   - Amlodipine 5 mg daily continued. BP 118/72 today, no changes needed.

7. Psychosocial/quality of life.
   - Patient expresses cautious optimism about her response to the trial. She has returned to part-time teaching, which is a source of significant personal fulfillment. She asked about the longer-term data on this trial combination — I reviewed the early signal from Phase I data and the rationale for the combination (dual EGFR/MET targeting to overcome bypass resistance and SOS1-mediated KRAS pathway activation). We discussed that durability data is still maturing and that continued monitoring is essential.
   - Patient's family members (husband and adult daughter) present today; all questions addressed. Social work referral offered and declined at this time.
   - Trial nurse coordinator contact information reinforced.

Return to clinic: In approximately 3-4 weeks for cycle 5 follow-up and review of 4-month restaging imaging. Earlier if new symptoms arise, particularly any neurological changes or infusion-related concerns.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Thoracic Oncology
Clinical Trial Principal Investigator
